titleStmt.title: Revisions to Henry David Thoreau's manuscript of Walden

publicationStmt.p: Transcriptions from HM 924, the manuscript of Walden: Brute Neighbors 9 Version A, Brute Neighbors 8 Version E, Brute Neighbors 9 Version E

sourceDesc.bibl: "Brute Neighbors" appears as early as Draft A of Walden, where it begins following "Spring" 26b. By Draft E this section has become paragraph 9, and Thoreau has added an additional 8 paragraphs before it. In the exerpts below we see Thoreau both cross out and add via pencil large sections of text. These changes demonstrate Thoreau adding additional reflections on the "neighbors" of Walden pond, as well as removing reflections for overall text coherence.

seg.add: When ploughing my field in the spring I found a ground bird's nest
directly in the path of my plow which would have been turned in
by the next furrow slice but though I took it up
carefully with the sod in which it rested & set it in the grass a few
rods off beyond the plowed land, where I had taken out another
sod of same size, yet the parents were more cruel
than I and when I looked again the young had been deserted and were
dead.
seg.note: At the top this page from A, Thoreau has penciled part of paragraph 9 between the lines of "Spring" 26b. Implication: after penning the rest of paragraph 9, Thoreau returned to add this encounter with the bird's nest. This may have been done to ease the transition between "Spring", which discussed birds, and the closer animal encounters of "Brute Neighbors" 9.

zone.seg:
A long-earedred bellied
field mouse had her nest underneath
my house, and before i had laid
the second floor, and swept out
the shavings, would come out to pick
up the crumbs at my feet when
I took my lunch. It had probably
never seen a man before, and soon
bexame quite familiar. It would
come out regularly at lunch time,
and run over my shoes and up my
clothing, and my legs inside cling-
ing to the flesh. It would run readily
up the sides of the room by short
impulses like a squirrel, which
it resembled considerably in its
motions. At length as I leaned

my elbow on the bench one day, it
ran up my clothes, and along my
sleeve, and round and round the
paper which held my dinner while I kept itthe latter close dodging and play-
inng at bo-peep with it, and when
at last I held still a piece of cheese
between my thumb and finger, it
came and nibbled it, sitting in
my hand, and hten cleaned its face
and paws like a fly, and walked
away. Thoreau describes two experiences that reveal different aspects of nature. The story of the bird nest suggests a struggle between human desire to help nature and our own lack of understanding of it. The story of the mouse demonstrates the intimate and trusting relationship humans can have with nature, as well as the way many things in nature are similar and connected (comparing the mouse to both a fly and a squirrel).

seg.del: thus strong in me, I was generally
of the brute creatures as were my
habits. Though I took up a
which was directly in the path of
seg.note: This section contains both new writing and what was once pencil text on version A of the manuscript. It seems as though Thoreau added a transition between the newly added paragraphs 1-7 and his musings on the bird's nest (hunter instincts to observing habits), before crossing it out and replacing it with paragraph 8 (seen below). The bird nest story was boxed in, separating it from this crossed out transition, and seems to have been crossed out at a later date when Thoreau scrapped it as well.

seg.add: Why do precisely these objects which we behold make a world?
Why has man just these species of animals for instance
for his neighbors & no others?
as if nothing but a mouse could have filled this crevice?
They must be very sig-
nificant though we fail to percieve their significance.
I suspect that
Pilpay & co.
have
put them to their best
use
Are they not all beasts of
burden in a sense
made to carry some portion
of our thoughts?
seg.note: This section was written in pencil between the lines of the (now deleted) start of paragraph 9. As noted above, it seems as though this section replaced the start of paragraph 9 as a transition to the bird nest story before that section was scrapped. Perhaps the bird nest was scrapped as this section transitions more seamlessly into the rest of paragraph 9 without it, as both mention a mouse.

seg.del: been turned in by the next
the sod and setting it in the
grass a few rods beyond the
plowed land, where I had taken
out another sod of the same
faithful than I, for when I
seg.note: Written in ink, then crossed out and interlined with pencil (see below).

seg.add: The mice which haunted my house were not the
common ones which are said to have been introduced
into the country - but a peculiar wild native kind
not found in the village - one which I sent to
a distinguished naturalist interested him much -

zone.seg:
When I was building my house
a long-eared red-bellied field-
mouse one of these had its nest underneath
it, and before I had laid
the second floor and swept
out the shavings, it would
come out regularly at dinnerlunch
time, and pick up the crumbs
at my feet. It probably had
never seen a man before and
soon became quite familiar,
and would run over my shoes
and up my clothes. It could
readily ascend the sides of the
room by short impulses like
a squirrel which it resembled in
its motion. At length, I leaned This segment is again carried over from version A. It appears that after penning this section, Thoreau decided to add more information about the kind of mice he was discussing. To avoid redundancy he replaced "a long-eared red-bellied field mouse" with "one of these", likely after adding the pencil text. Perhaps the addition of referencing the peculiarity of the mouse, as well as the interest of a naturalist friend, was intended to highlight how special the nature of Walden pond was, and how it differed from Thoreau's previous natural experiences.

my elbow on the bench one
one day, it ran up my clothes
and along my sleeve, and
round and round, my paper
which held my dinner, while
I kept the latter close, and dodged
and played at bo-peep with it;
and when at last I held still
a piece of cheese between my thumb and
finger, it came and nib-
bled it, sitting in my hand,
and afterward cleaned its face
and paws like a fly, and walked
away.
